summary:
The Nok Culture (c. 500–200 BC) of central Nigeria was the first known iron-smelting society in sub-Saharan Africa and produced remarkable terracotta figurines — the earliest large-scale sculptural tradition in sub-Saharan Africa — representing a level of artistic and technological sophistication that has transformed understanding of ancient African civilizations.

The Nok people, whose precise social organization and language remain largely unknown, settled the Jos Plateau region of modern Nigeria. Their terracotta sculptures — typically hollow cylindrical heads with distinctive triangular or D-shaped eyes, elaborate hairstyles, and detailed jewelry representations — were made at a scale and with a technical mastery unprecedented in the region. The figures may represent ancestors, deities, or notable individuals. Their iron-smelting technology, possibly developed independently rather than diffused from North Africa, gave Nok society military and agricultural advantages. The culture appears to have ended abruptly around 200 BC; its causes are unknown. The Nok terracottas, discovered by tin miners in the 1930s, have been heavily looted in recent decades, creating a significant cultural heritage crisis.
